title: Dog Scuffing Toenails When Walking: Causes and Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: Is your dog dragging their paws? Learn why dogs scuff their toenails, the urgency level of this symptom, and how to help your vet diagnose the cause.
content_html:
TL;DR: Toenail scuffing is often a sign of underlying neurological or orthopedic issues that require veterinary attention. While typically chronic, seek emergency care if scuffing is accompanied by sudden paralysis, severe pain, or loss of bladder control.

What is toenail scuffing and why does it happen to dogs?

Scuffing or dragging the toenails—often called 'knuckling'—occurs when a dog does not fully lift their paws while moving. This leads to the nails scraping against the ground, which can cause them to wear down unevenly or bleed. This behavior is typically a sign of an underlying issue affecting the dog's proprioception (their awareness of where their limbs are in space) or physical strength.

When is toenail scuffing considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Chronic or gradual scuffing is generally low urgency but indicates progressive conditions like arthritis, Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), or neurological decline.
  • Scuffing should not be ignored even if it seems minor.
  • Contact an emergency vet immediately if the scuffing is accompanied by a sudden inability to walk, extreme pain, or loss of bladder control.

How can photos and videos of my dog help the vet during triage?

  • A video of your dog walking at home captures their natural gait without the influence of clinic-induced adrenaline.
  • Footage of your dog’s gait on a flat, non-slip surface helps your veterinarian see the frequency and severity of the scuffing.
  • Close-up photos of the toenails show specific wear patterns that help distinguish between orthopedic pain and neurological deficits.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Locomotor difficulties can arise from musculoskeletal or neurological conditions. When evaluating a dog that is scuffing its toenails, examination should include assessment for abnormal wear of the claws, which may indicate the source of the lameness.

Chapter: Musculoskeletal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1067)

faqs.question: Why are only my dog's back nails wearing down?
faqs.answer: This is often a sign of issues in the lower spine or hips, such as hip dysplasia or degenerative myelopathy, which affect the hind limbs' coordination.

faqs.question: Can boots help with nail scuffing?
faqs.answer: Protective boots can prevent the nails from wearing down to the quick and keep the paws safe from scrapes, but they do not treat the underlying cause of the dragging.

faqs.question: Is scuffing just a normal part of aging?
faqs.answer: While common in senior dogs, it is not considered 'normal.' It usually indicates manageable discomfort or a neurological condition that requires a veterinary diagnosis.
